summary: A catastrophically misidentifies the algorithm as CNN Max Pooling—an unrelated concept—and provides no kernel substance. B misnames it Newton-Raphson but at least narrates pairwise averaging correctly and partially addresses threadgroup sizing and shared memory. Neither mentions threadgroup_barrier or log₂(N) complexity. B is the less wrong answer.

Both answers are truncated mid-response. Each correctly applies the 50% resolution ceiling to derive Route A = 36% enantiopure yield. Neither completes Route B's calculation (~39.4%), names the sunk cost fallacy, dismisses the $350k as irrelevant, or addresses R&D misallocation. Coverage and error counts are effectively identical.

A fails entirely on archaic register and incorrectly describes zeroing; its verification step is truncated. B correctly uses archaic second-person forms, avoids anachronistic vocabulary, and opens homing properly, but truncates before completing encoder-zeroing and limit-verification phases, omitting two required steps.

Both answers are severely deficient. A is truncated and only restates what the pronoun mappings do without identifying any bug. B at least gestures at irregular verb handling and contractions, but neither answer names hath/doth, identifies -est false positives on 'best'/'forest', or catches the thine→yours predicate-position failure.
